exact sample likelihoods. We experimentally validate our approach in data-free
Combinatorial Optimization and demonstrate that our method achieves a new
state-of-the-art on a wide range of benchmark problems.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper introduces DiffUCO, a diffusion model framework for unsupervised neural combinatorial optimization. It addresses the challenge of sampling from intractable distributions over discrete sets without training data by using an upper bound on the reverse Kullback-Leibler divergence as a loss function. The method achieves state-of-the-art performance on various combinatorial optimization benchmarks.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.methodology: DiffUCO employs a diffusion model to approximate the target distribution by iteratively sampling from a noise distribution in the forward process and learning the reverse process to map samples back to the target distribution. The model uses a Joint Variational Upper Bound as a loss function and can be used with categorical or annealed noise distributions. Conditional Expectation (CE) and Subgraph Tokenization (ST) are used to improve solution quality and sampling efficiency.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The method is evaluated on Maximum Independent Set (MIS), Maximum Clique (MaxCl), Minimum Dominating Set (MDS), Maximum Cut (MaxCut), and Minimum Vertex Cover (MVC) problems. The models are trained and tested on RB and Barabasi-Albert (BA) graphs. Performance is compared against other unsupervised learning methods and optimal solvers like Gurobi. Metrics include relative error and approximation ratio.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.limitations: The learned probability distribution may have a bias, leading to samples that do not perfectly follow the target distribution. Training DiffUCO on large graph datasets with high connectivity can be memory- and time-expensive.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research could focus on reducing memory and time costs, potentially by reducing the computational graph during backpropagation. Exploring latent diffusion models and U-Net inspired GNN architectures are also suggested. Additionally, improving the model to generate unbiased samples from the target distribution is mentioned.

candidate_base_papers_info_list.title:
Adaptive Learning of the Latent Space of Wasserstein Generative
  Adversarial Networks
candidate_base_papers_info_list.summary:
Generative models based on latent variables, such as generative adversarial
networks (GANs) and variational auto-encoders (VAEs), have gained lots of
interests due to their impressive performance in many fields. However, many
data such as natural images usually do not populate the ambient Euclidean space
but instead reside in a lower-dimensional manifold. Thus an inappropriate
choice of the latent dimension fails to uncover the structure of the data,
possibly resulting in mismatch of latent representations and poor generative
qualities. Towards addressing these problems, we propose a novel framework
called the latent Wasserstein GAN (LWGAN) that fuses the Wasserstein
auto-encoder and the Wasserstein GAN so that the intrinsic dimension of the
data manifold can be adaptively learned by a modified informative latent
distribution. We prove that there exist an encoder network and a generator
network in such a way that the intrinsic dimension of the learned encoding
distribution is equal to the dimension of the data manifold. We theoretically
establish that our estimated intrinsic dimension is a consistent estimate of
the true dimension of the data manifold. Meanwhile, we provide an upper bound
on the generalization error of LWGAN, implying that we force the synthetic data
distribution to be similar to the real data distribution from a population
perspective. Comprehensive empirical experiments verify our framework and show
that LWGAN is able to identify the correct intrinsic dimension under several
scenarios, and simultaneously generate high-quality synthetic data by sampling
from the learned latent distribution.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper addresses the problem of mismatch between the latent dimension and the data manifold in generative models like GANs and VAEs. It proposes a novel framework called Latent Wasserstein GAN (LWGAN) that adaptively learns the intrinsic dimension of the data manifold by fusing Wasserstein auto-encoders and Wasserstein GANs. The paper proves the existence of encoder and generator networks where the learned encoding distribution's intrinsic dimension equals the data manifold's dimension, establishes estimation consistency for the intrinsic dimension, and provides a generalization error bound.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.methodology: LWGAN combines Wasserstein Auto-Encoders (WAE) and Wasserstein GANs (WGAN). It modifies the latent distribution to a generalized normal distribution N(0, A), where A is a diagonal matrix with 0 or 1 entries, allowing the rank of A to characterize the intrinsic dimension. A deterministic encoder Q learns an informative prior distribution, and a generator G generates images using the latent code Z. The framework uses the 1-Wasserstein distance to measure similarities between distributions.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The framework is validated using simulated experiments on synthetic datasets like S-curves, Swiss rolls, and Hyperplanes. It is also evaluated on real image datasets like MNIST and CelebA. Performance is measured using metrics like Inception Score (IS), Fréchet Inception Distance (FID), and reconstruction error. The method involves training neural networks for the encoder, generator, and critic, with specific architectures detailed in the supplementary material.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.limitations: The paper assumes the existence of a homeomorphism between the data manifold and a Euclidean space. The 1-Lipschitz constraint on the critic function is enforced using the gradient penalty technique, which has its own set of tuning requirements. The practical implementation relies on neural networks, which are known to be highly overparameterized, making it difficult to interpret the rank parameter as the number of parameters in the model.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research directions include investigating a more general scenario where the generator G is stochastic by adding extra noise to the input. It also suggests incorporating the stochastic LWGAN into more recent GAN modules like BigGAN to produce high-resolution and high-fidelity images along with the estimation of the intrinsic dimension. Furthermore, it proposes applications of LWGAN for structural estimation in economics by learning data representations and boosting sample size.

candidate_base_papers_info_list.title: Diffusion Model-Augmented Behavioral Cloning
candidate_base_papers_info_list.summary:
Imitation learning addresses the challenge of learning by observing an
expert's demonstrations without access to reward signals from environments.
Most existing imitation learning methods that do not require interacting with
environments either model the expert distribution as the conditional
probability p(a|s) (e.g., behavioral cloning, BC) or the joint probability p(s,
a). Despite the simplicity of modeling the conditional probability with BC, it
usually struggles with generalization. While modeling the joint probability can
improve generalization performance, the inference procedure is often
time-consuming, and the model can suffer from manifold overfitting. This work
proposes an imitation learning framework that benefits from modeling both the
conditional and joint probability of the expert distribution. Our proposed
Diffusion Model-Augmented Behavioral Cloning (DBC) employs a diffusion model
trained to model expert behaviors and learns a policy to optimize both the BC
loss (conditional) and our proposed diffusion model loss (joint). DBC
outperforms baselines in various continuous control tasks in navigation, robot
arm manipulation, dexterous manipulation, and locomotion. We design additional
experiments to verify the limitations of modeling either the conditional
probability or the joint probability of the expert distribution, as well as
compare different generative models. Ablation studies justify the effectiveness
of our design choices.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper introduces Diffusion Model-Augmented Behavioral Cloning (DBC), a novel imitation learning framework. DBC combines behavioral cloning (BC) with a diffusion model to leverage both the conditional probability p(a|s) and joint probability p(s, a) of expert demonstrations. This approach aims to improve generalization in imitation learning tasks by addressing the limitations of solely modeling conditional probabilities with BC or encountering manifold overfitting when modeling joint probabilities.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.methodology: DBC employs a diffusion model trained to capture the joint probability of expert state-action pairs. It learns a policy by optimizing a combined loss function comprising the standard BC loss and a diffusion model loss. The diffusion model loss guides policy learning by providing an estimate of how well the predicted actions align with the expert's joint distribution. The method uses Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPMs) to model expert state-action pairs.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The framework was evaluated on continuous control tasks, including navigation (MAZE), robot arm manipulation (FETCH PICK), dexterous manipulation (HAND ROTATE), and locomotion (CHEETAH, WALKER, ANTREACH). Performance was measured using success rate and return. Baselines included BC, Implicit BC (IBC), and Diffusion Policy. Ablation studies were conducted to assess the impact of different generative models and hyperparameters. Generalization experiments were performed in FETCH PICK by injecting noise into initial states and goal locations.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.limitations: The proposed framework is designed to learn from expert trajectories without interacting with the environment, which means it cannot learn from agent trajectories. The framework inherits any biases exhibited by the expert demonstrators, which can manifest as sub-optimal, unsafe, or even discriminatory behaviors.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research directions include extending the method to incorporate agent data, which could potentially allow for improvement when interacting with environments is possible. Mitigating bias and promoting fairness in imitation learning is also identified as an important area for future work.

candidate_base_papers_info_list.title:
Consistent Diffusion Meets Tweedie: Training Exact Ambient Diffusion
  Models with Noisy Data
candidate_base_papers_info_list.summary:
Ambient diffusion is a recently proposed framework for training diffusion
models using corrupted data. Both Ambient Diffusion and alternative SURE-based
approaches for learning diffusion models from corrupted data resort to
approximations which deteriorate performance. We present the first framework
for training diffusion models that provably sample from the uncorrupted
distribution given only noisy training data, solving an open problem in this
space. Our key technical contribution is a method that uses a double
application of Tweedie's formula and a consistency loss function that allows us
to extend sampling at noise levels below the observed data noise. We also
provide further evidence that diffusion models memorize from their training
sets by identifying extremely corrupted images that are almost perfectly
reconstructed, raising copyright and privacy concerns. Our method for training
using corrupted samples can be used to mitigate this problem. We demonstrate
this by fine-tuning Stable Diffusion XL to generate samples from a distribution
using only noisy samples. Our framework reduces the amount of memorization of
the fine-tuning dataset, while maintaining competitive performance.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.main_contributions: This paper introduces the first exact framework for training diffusion models using only corrupted samples, addressing an open problem in Ambient Diffusion. Key contributions include a method using a double application of Tweedie's formula and a consistency loss function to extend sampling at noise levels below the observed data noise. The paper also provides evidence of diffusion models memorizing training sets and uses the framework to fine-tune Stable Diffusion XL, reducing memorization while maintaining performance.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.methodology: The methodology involves applying Tweedie's formula twice to learn optimal denoisers for noise levels above that of the training data. A consistency loss function is then used to learn optimal denoisers for noise levels below that of the training data. This allows for training diffusion models using corrupted samples without approximations.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: Experiments were conducted using Stable Diffusion XL (SDXL) on the LAION dataset and FFHQ dataset. The models were fine-tuned on FFHQ at 1024x1024 resolution, with varying noise levels. Memorization was evaluated by measuring the similarity between generated samples and their nearest neighbors in the training data using DINOv2 embeddings. FID score was used to measure the performance of unconditional generation.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.limitations: The method does not solve the problem of training diffusion models with linearly corrupted data that provably sample from the uncorrupted distribution. Training with consistency increases training time. The proposed Ambient Denoising Score Matching objective did not work on very limited datasets (<100 samples).
candidate_base_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research directions include exploring training diffusion models with linearly corrupted data and addressing the limitations observed on very limited datasets. Further exploration of the trade-offs between training time and performance when using consistency loss is also suggested.

candidate_base_papers_info_list.title: DiffDA: a Diffusion Model for Weather-scale Data Assimilation
candidate_base_papers_info_list.summary:
The generation of initial conditions via accurate data assimilation is
crucial for weather forecasting and climate modeling. We propose DiffDA as a
denoising diffusion model capable of assimilating atmospheric variables using
predicted states and sparse observations. Acknowledging the similarity between
a weather forecast model and a denoising diffusion model dedicated to weather
applications, we adapt the pretrained GraphCast neural network as the backbone
of the diffusion model. Through experiments based on simulated observations
from the ERA5 reanalysis dataset, our method can produce assimilated global
atmospheric data consistent with observations at 0.25 deg (~30km) resolution
globally. This marks the highest resolution achieved by ML data assimilation
models. The experiments also show that the initial conditions assimilated from
sparse observations (less than 0.96% of gridded data) and 48-hour forecast can
be used for forecast models with a loss of lead time of at most 24 hours
compared to initial conditions from state-of-the-art data assimilation in ERA5.
This enables the application of the method to real-world applications, such as
creating reanalysis datasets with autoregressive data assimilation.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper introduces DiffDA, a novel machine learning method for weather-scale data assimilation. It utilizes a denoising diffusion model with the GraphCast neural network as its backbone to assimilate high-resolution atmospheric data, achieving a resolution of 0.25◦. The method demonstrates data assimilation cycles by combining DiffDA with an ML weather forecasting model, creating reanalysis datasets consistent with observations. The assimilated data can be used for weather forecasting applications.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.methodology: The method uses a denoising diffusion probabilistic model (DDPM) to sample from a conditional distribution of atmospheric states conditioned on observations and predicted states. It adapts the pre-trained GraphCast model as the backbone of the diffusion model. Conditioning for sparse observations is handled using inpainting techniques with a soft mask and interpolated observations. A skip connection is added from the predicted state to the de-normalized model output.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The experiments are based on simulated observations from the ERA5 reanalysis dataset, using the WeatherBench2 dataset for training and validation. The model is trained on 48 NVIDIA A100 GPUs. The method is evaluated through single-step data assimilation, autoregressive data assimilation, and forecast experiments, comparing the results against ERA5 data and GraphCast forecasts. Root mean square errors (RMSEs) are used as the primary evaluation metric.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.limitations: The method currently only supports point measurements at a single time step as observations, excluding satellite imagery and radar soundings. It also lacks quality control of the input observation data, relying on simulated observations from the ERA5 dataset. The method struggles to assimilate 2m temperature (t2m) in autoregressive data assimilation experiments.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research directions include supporting broader types of observations and multiple time steps using loss-function-based conditioning and exploring the addition of satellite imagery conditioning directly into the diffusion model. The authors also plan to investigate possible quality control techniques for the input observation data and explore four-dimensional assimilation to improve accuracy and stabilize the temporal roll out.

selected_base_paper_info.title:
Purify++: Improving Diffusion-Purification with Advanced Diffusion
  Models and Control of Randomness
selected_base_paper_info.summary:
Adversarial attacks can mislead neural network classifiers. The defense
against adversarial attacks is important for AI safety. Adversarial
purification is a family of approaches that defend adversarial attacks with
suitable pre-processing. Diffusion models have been shown to be effective for
adversarial purification. Despite their success, many aspects of diffusion
purification still remain unexplored. In this paper, we investigate and improve
upon three limiting designs of diffusion purification: the use of an improved
diffusion model, advanced numerical simulation techniques, and optimal control
of randomness. Based on our findings, we propose Purify++, a new diffusion
purification algorithm that is now the state-of-the-art purification method
against several adversarial attacks. Our work presents a systematic exploration
of the limits of diffusion purification methods.
selected_base_paper_info.main_contributions: The paper introduces Purify++, a new diffusion purification algorithm for defending against adversarial attacks on neural networks. It improves upon existing diffusion purification methods by using an improved diffusion model (EDM), advanced numerical simulation techniques (Heun's method), and optimal control of randomness, achieving state-of-the-art purification performance.
selected_base_paper_info.methodology: The core methodology involves pre-processing input data with a forward-and-reverse procedure using diffusion models. The forward diffusion adds noise to adversarial samples, and the reverse process (purification) removes the noise using numerical simulation of purification SDE or ODE. The key improvements focus on using Variance Exploding (VE) diffusion (specifically, EDM) instead of Variance Preserving (VP) diffusion, employing Heun's method for efficient simulation, and controlling the strength of randomness in the purification procedure by combining purification ODE and Langevin dynamics.
selected_base_paper_info.experimental_setup: The experiments were conducted on MNIST and CIFAR10 datasets. The performance of Purify++ was evaluated against black-box (Square Attack, SPSA), gray-box (PGD), and adaptive (BPDA+EOT) attacks. Standard and robust accuracy were used as evaluation metrics. The method was compared with existing adversarial training and purification methods, including DiffPure. Ablation studies were performed to quantify the contribution of each component of Purify++.
selected_base_paper_info.limitations: The paper acknowledges the high computational cost associated with strong adaptive attacks like BPDA+EOT, which limits the evaluation to a subset of the test set. Also, some experimental results are marked as being obtained on a subset from the whole test dataset. Further, the specific conditions under which the theoretical guarantees hold for diffusion purification are only mentioned generally.
selected_base_paper_info.future_research_directions: The paper suggests further developments in diffusion-based purification methods, implying potential research directions in exploring new diffusion models, simulation techniques, and randomness control strategies. A specific direction is not explicitly stated but is implied through the conclusion.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title:
Consistent Diffusion Meets Tweedie: Training Exact Ambient Diffusion
  Models with Noisy Data
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
Ambient diffusion is a recently proposed framework for training diffusion
models using corrupted data. Both Ambient Diffusion and alternative SURE-based
approaches for learning diffusion models from corrupted data resort to
approximations which deteriorate performance. We present the first framework
for training diffusion models that provably sample from the uncorrupted
distribution given only noisy training data, solving an open problem in this
space. Our key technical contribution is a method that uses a double
application of Tweedie's formula and a consistency loss function that allows us
to extend sampling at noise levels below the observed data noise. We also
provide further evidence that diffusion models memorize from their training
sets by identifying extremely corrupted images that are almost perfectly
reconstructed, raising copyright and privacy concerns. Our method for training
using corrupted samples can be used to mitigate this problem. We demonstrate
this by fine-tuning Stable Diffusion XL to generate samples from a distribution
using only noisy samples. Our framework reduces the amount of memorization of
the fine-tuning dataset, while maintaining competitive performance.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper introduces a framework for training diffusion models using only corrupted samples, addressing an open problem in Ambient Diffusion. Key contributions include a method using a double application of Tweedie's formula and a consistency loss function to enable sampling at noise levels below the observed data noise. The research also provides evidence of diffusion models memorizing training sets and uses corrupted samples to mitigate this, fine-tuning Stable Diffusion XL to generate samples from a distribution using only noisy samples and reducing memorization.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: The methodology involves a double application of Tweedie's formula to learn optimal denoisers for noise levels above that of the training data and a consistency loss function to learn optimal denoisers for noise levels below that of the training data. The approach trains the network to predict the denoised image by removing additional noise introduced to the given samples, using a Stochastic Differential Equation for sampling.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: Experiments were conducted using Stable Diffusion XL (SDXL) on a subset of the LAION dataset and FFHQ dataset. The models were fine-tuned on corrupted data with varying noise levels. Evaluation metrics included image similarity scores using DINOv2 embeddings and FID score, as well as latent and pixel MSE for restoration performance.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The method does not solve the problem of training diffusion models with linearly corrupted data that provably sample from the uncorrupted distribution. Training with consistency increases training time. In preliminary experiments on very limited datasets (< 100 samples), the proposed Ambient Denoising Score Matching objective did not work.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research directions include exploring training diffusion models with linearly corrupted data that provably sample from the uncorrupted distribution, reducing the training time associated with consistency training, and addressing the limitations observed on very limited datasets.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title:
A Diffusion Model Framework for Unsupervised Neural Combinatorial
  Optimization
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
Learning to sample from intractable distributions over discrete sets without
relying on corresponding training data is a central problem in a wide range of
fields, including Combinatorial Optimization. Currently, popular deep
learning-based approaches rely primarily on generative models that yield exact
sample likelihoods. This work introduces a method that lifts this restriction
and opens the possibility to employ highly expressive latent variable models
like diffusion models. Our approach is conceptually based on a loss that upper
bounds the reverse Kullback-Leibler divergence and evades the requirement of
exact sample likelihoods. We experimentally validate our approach in data-free
Combinatorial Optimization and demonstrate that our method achieves a new
state-of-the-art on a wide range of benchmark problems.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: This paper introduces DiffUCO, a diffusion model framework for unsupervised neural combinatorial optimization. The key contribution is a novel Joint Variational Upper Bound loss function that allows the application of latent variable models like diffusion models to data-free approximation of discrete distributions, achieving state-of-the-art performance on various combinatorial optimization problems.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: The methodology involves training a diffusion model to approximate the Boltzmann distribution associated with a combinatorial optimization problem. The model uses a forward diffusion process to transform the target distribution into a stationary distribution, and a reverse diffusion process, modeled by the neural network, to map samples from the stationary distribution back to the target distribution. The training is based on minimizing a Joint Variational Upper Bound on the reverse Kullback-Leibler divergence. Conditional Expectation (CE) and Subgraph Tokenization (ST) are also used to improve solution quality and efficiency.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The method is evaluated on five different CO problem types: Maximum Independent Set (MIS), Maximum Clique (MaxCl), Minimum Dominating Set (MDS), Maximum Cut (MaxCut), and Minimum Vertex Cover (MVC). The experiments use RB-model graphs for MIS and MaxCl, and Barabasi-Albert (BA) graphs for MDS and MaxCut. Performance is compared against state-of-the-art autoregressive models and classical solvers like Gurobi. Metrics include relative error and approximation ratio. Ablation studies are performed to assess the impact of diffusion steps and noise distributions.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The learned probability distribution has a bias and does not exactly follow the target distribution. Training DiffUCO on large graph datasets with high connectivity is memory- and time-expensive.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research directions include improving memory and time efficiency by reducing the computational graph during backpropagation, exploring latent diffusion models and U-Net inspired GNN architectures, and developing methods that allow to obtain unbiased samples from the target distribution.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title:
Purify++: Improving Diffusion-Purification with Advanced Diffusion
  Models and Control of Randomness
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
Adversarial attacks can mislead neural network classifiers. The defense
against adversarial attacks is important for AI safety. Adversarial
purification is a family of approaches that defend adversarial attacks with
suitable pre-processing. Diffusion models have been shown to be effective for
adversarial purification. Despite their success, many aspects of diffusion
purification still remain unexplored. In this paper, we investigate and improve
upon three limiting designs of diffusion purification: the use of an improved
diffusion model, advanced numerical simulation techniques, and optimal control
of randomness. Based on our findings, we propose Purify++, a new diffusion
purification algorithm that is now the state-of-the-art purification method
against several adversarial attacks. Our work presents a systematic exploration
of the limits of diffusion purification methods.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper introduces Purify++, a new diffusion purification algorithm for defending against adversarial attacks on neural networks. It improves upon existing diffusion purification methods by using an improved diffusion model, advanced numerical simulation techniques, and optimal control of randomness, achieving state-of-the-art purification performance.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: Purify++ employs an improved diffusion model (EDM, a generalization of VE diffusion), efficient purification simulations using Heun's method, and optimal control of randomness achieved by combining purification ODE and Langevin dynamics, controlled by a mixing coefficient.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The method is evaluated on MNIST and CIFAR10 datasets using various adversarial attacks, including black-box (Square Attack, SPSA), gray-box (PGD), and adaptive attacks (BPDA+EOT). Performance is measured using standard and robust accuracy, comparing against existing adversarial defense methods.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The evaluation of the adaptive attack (BPDA+EOT) is performed on a subset of the CIFAR10 test set due to high computational costs. The paper also notes the independent training of the diffusion model and classifier on the same training dataset as previous works, potentially limiting exploration of joint training strategies.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: The paper suggests further developments in diffusion-based purification methods, particularly exploring different architectures and sampling techniques of diffusion models. It also hints at the potential for exploring joint training strategies of the diffusion model and classifier.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title:
Adaptive Learning of the Latent Space of Wasserstein Generative
  Adversarial Networks
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
Generative models based on latent variables, such as generative adversarial
networks (GANs) and variational auto-encoders (VAEs), have gained lots of
interests due to their impressive performance in many fields. However, many
data such as natural images usually do not populate the ambient Euclidean space
but instead reside in a lower-dimensional manifold. Thus an inappropriate
choice of the latent dimension fails to uncover the structure of the data,
possibly resulting in mismatch of latent representations and poor generative
qualities. Towards addressing these problems, we propose a novel framework
called the latent Wasserstein GAN (LWGAN) that fuses the Wasserstein
auto-encoder and the Wasserstein GAN so that the intrinsic dimension of the
data manifold can be adaptively learned by a modified informative latent
distribution. We prove that there exist an encoder network and a generator
network in such a way that the intrinsic dimension of the learned encoding
distribution is equal to the dimension of the data manifold. We theoretically
establish that our estimated intrinsic dimension is a consistent estimate of
the true dimension of the data manifold. Meanwhile, we provide an upper bound
on the generalization error of LWGAN, implying that we force the synthetic data
distribution to be similar to the real data distribution from a population
perspective. Comprehensive empirical experiments verify our framework and show
that LWGAN is able to identify the correct intrinsic dimension under several
scenarios, and simultaneously generate high-quality synthetic data by sampling
from the learned latent distribution.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper proposes a novel framework called Latent Wasserstein GAN (LWGAN) to adaptively learn the intrinsic dimension of data distributions supported on manifolds. LWGAN fuses the Wasserstein auto-encoder and the Wasserstein GAN to improve generative modeling and representation learning by learning a modified informative latent distribution. The paper provides theoretical guarantees on the generalization error bound, estimation consistency, and intrinsic dimension consistency.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: LWGAN combines WGAN and WAE in a principled way. It changes the latent distribution from N(0, Id) to a generalized normal distribution N(0, A), where A is a diagonal matrix with entries taking values 0 or 1, allowing the rank of A to characterize the intrinsic dimension of the latent space. A deterministic encoder Q: X -> Z learns an informative prior distribution PZ ~ N(0, A), and a generator G: Z -> X generates images using the latent code Z from PZ. The 1-Wasserstein distance measures the similarities between distributions.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The framework is verified using simulated experiments on toy examples (S-curve, Swiss roll, Hyperplane) and real image data (MNIST, CelebA). The performance is evaluated based on the ability to detect the correct intrinsic dimension, generate high-quality samples, and obtain small reconstruction errors. Metrics such as Inception Score (IS), Fréchet Inception Distance (FID), and reconstruction error are used.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The paper focuses on a deterministic generator G. The choice of the regularization parameter λn in the rank-regularized objective function requires careful tuning, although a practical data-driven scheme is proposed. The neural network function space G × Q × F needs to be large enough to cover some optimal points of interest, and the theoretical rates for model selection consistency differ from those of BIC.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research directions include investigating a more general scenario where the generator G is stochastic by adding an extra noise vector to its input. Another direction involves incorporating the stochastic LWGAN into more recent GAN modules like BigGAN to produce high-resolution and high-fidelity images along with intrinsic dimension estimation. LWGAN can also be applied to structural estimation in econometrics by adaptively learning data representation and boosting sample size.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title: Hyperbolic Geometric Latent Diffusion Model for Graph Generation
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
Diffusion models have made significant contributions to computer vision,
sparking a growing interest in the community recently regarding the application
of them to graph generation. Existing discrete graph diffusion models exhibit
heightened computational complexity and diminished training efficiency. A
preferable and natural way is to directly diffuse the graph within the latent
space. However, due to the non-Euclidean structure of graphs is not isotropic
in the latent space, the existing latent diffusion models effectively make it
difficult to capture and preserve the topological information of graphs. To
address the above challenges, we propose a novel geometrically latent diffusion
framework HypDiff. Specifically, we first establish a geometrically latent
space with interpretability measures based on hyperbolic geometry, to define
anisotropic latent diffusion processes for graphs. Then, we propose a
geometrically latent diffusion process that is constrained by both radial and
angular geometric properties, thereby ensuring the preservation of the original
topological properties in the generative graphs. Extensive experimental results
demonstrate the superior effectiveness of HypDiff for graph generation with
various topologies.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper introduces HypDiff, a novel hyperbolic geometric latent diffusion model for graph generation that addresses the anisotropy of non-Euclidean structures. It proposes a geometrically latent diffusion process based on radial and angular geometric constraints in hyperbolic space to preserve topological properties and improve generation quality.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: The HypDiff model uses a hyperbolic autoencoder to embed graphs into a hyperbolic space. It then applies a geometrically latent diffusion process with radial and angular constraints. The radial constraint addresses the additivity issue of Gaussian distributions in hyperbolic space, while the angular constraint preserves structural prior during diffusion. A denoising network based on UNET is trained to reverse the diffusion process.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The model was evaluated on synthetic datasets (SBM, BA, Community, Ego, BA-G, Grid) and real-world datasets (Cora, Citeseer, Polblogs, MUTAG, IMDB-B, PROTEINS, COLLAB) for node classification and graph generation tasks. Performance was measured using F1 scores for node classification and Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) and F1 scores (precision-recall, density-coverage) for graph generation. Baselines included Euclidean and hyperbolic graph representation learning methods, deep graph generative models, and graph diffusion generative models.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The paper does not explicitly state limitations, but it can be inferred that the model's performance might be sensitive to the choice of hyperbolic curvature and clustering parameters. Additionally, the approximation of Gaussian distribution in hyperbolic space with tangent plane Gaussian distribution could introduce inaccuracies. The complexity analysis suggests potential scalability issues with very large graphs.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research could explore adaptive selection of hyperbolic curvature and clustering parameters, investigate more accurate approximations of Gaussian distributions in hyperbolic space, and extend the model to handle dynamic graphs or graphs with node/edge features. Applying HypDiff to other graph-related tasks, such as graph classification or link prediction, could also be a promising direction.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title: Prompt-guided Precise Audio Editing with Diffusion Models
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
Audio editing involves the arbitrary manipulation of audio content through
precise control. Although text-guided diffusion models have made significant
advancements in text-to-audio generation, they still face challenges in finding
a flexible and precise way to modify target events within an audio track. We
present a novel approach, referred to as PPAE, which serves as a general module
for diffusion models and enables precise audio editing. The editing is based on
the input textual prompt only and is entirely training-free. We exploit the
cross-attention maps of diffusion models to facilitate accurate local editing
and employ a hierarchical local-global pipeline to ensure a smoother editing
process. Experimental results highlight the effectiveness of our method in
various editing tasks.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper introduces Prompt-guided Precise Audio Editing (PPAE), a training-free approach for precise audio editing using diffusion models. PPAE manipulates the cross-attention maps of diffusion models to achieve accurate local editing, enabling modifications to target audio events while preserving unrelated parts. The method is presented as a flexible audio editing interface and a plug-in module for various diffusion models.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: PPAE leverages the cross-attention layer of diffusion models, where text and audio features are interconnected. It employs a hierarchical local-global pipeline, using a Fuser module for integrating attention maps and a bootstrapping method to adjust the guidance scale. The method also involves inverting the original audio into edit-friendly noise spaces.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The method was evaluated on three audio editing tasks: Audio Replace, Audio Refine, and Audio Reweight, using a cleaned subset of the Fsd50k dataset. Objective metrics included Fréchet distance (FD), Fréchet audio distance (FAD), Spectral distance (SD), Kullback–Leibler (KL) divergence, and CLAP Score. Subjective metrics involved Relevance and Consistency, assessed by human participants.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The method relies on accurate inversion of the given audio, and performance degrades if the audio content is outside the model's training domain. The approach primarily focuses on modifications at the attention map level, which inherently restricts the extent of the edits and may not be suitable for substantial structural alterations. The need to select optimal hyperparameters for different audio editing tasks is also a potential limitation.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research directions include enhancing the overall audio quality in complex editing scenarios, improving the processing time for real-time applications, and exploring mechanisms for ensuring the ethical use of PPAE, including safeguards against misuse for creating misleading or harmful content. Further work could also focus on structural level edits beyond attention map manipulation.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title:
Consistent Diffusion Meets Tweedie: Training Exact Ambient Diffusion
  Models with Noisy Data
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
Ambient diffusion is a recently proposed framework for training diffusion
models using corrupted data. Both Ambient Diffusion and alternative SURE-based
approaches for learning diffusion models from corrupted data resort to
approximations which deteriorate performance. We present the first framework
for training diffusion models that provably sample from the uncorrupted
distribution given only noisy training data, solving an open problem in this
space. Our key technical contribution is a method that uses a double
application of Tweedie's formula and a consistency loss function that allows us
to extend sampling at noise levels below the observed data noise. We also
provide further evidence that diffusion models memorize from their training
sets by identifying extremely corrupted images that are almost perfectly
reconstructed, raising copyright and privacy concerns. Our method for training
using corrupted samples can be used to mitigate this problem. We demonstrate
this by fine-tuning Stable Diffusion XL to generate samples from a distribution
using only noisy samples. Our framework reduces the amount of memorization of
the fine-tuning dataset, while maintaining competitive performance.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper introduces a framework for training diffusion models using only corrupted samples, addressing an open problem in Ambient Diffusion. Key contributions include a method using a double application of Tweedie's formula and a consistency loss function to enable sampling at noise levels below the observed data noise. The research also provides evidence of diffusion models memorizing training sets and uses corrupted samples to mitigate this, fine-tuning Stable Diffusion XL to generate samples from a distribution using only noisy samples and reducing memorization.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: The methodology involves a double application of Tweedie's formula to learn optimal denoisers for noise levels above that of the training data and a consistency loss function to learn optimal denoisers for noise levels below that of the training data. The approach trains the network to predict the denoised image by removing additional noise introduced to the given samples, using a Stochastic Differential Equation for sampling.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: Experiments were conducted using Stable Diffusion XL (SDXL) on a subset of the LAION dataset and FFHQ dataset. The models were fine-tuned on corrupted data with varying noise levels. Evaluation metrics included image similarity scores using DINOv2 embeddings and FID score, as well as latent and pixel MSE for restoration performance.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The method does not solve the problem of training diffusion models with linearly corrupted data that provably sample from the uncorrupted distribution. Training with consistency increases training time. In preliminary experiments on very limited datasets (< 100 samples), the proposed Ambient Denoising Score Matching objective did not work.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research directions include exploring training diffusion models with linearly corrupted data that provably sample from the uncorrupted distribution, reducing the training time associated with consistency training, and addressing the limitations observed on very limited datasets.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title:
A Diffusion Model Framework for Unsupervised Neural Combinatorial
  Optimization
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
Learning to sample from intractable distributions over discrete sets without
relying on corresponding training data is a central problem in a wide range of
fields, including Combinatorial Optimization. Currently, popular deep
learning-based approaches rely primarily on generative models that yield exact
sample likelihoods. This work introduces a method that lifts this restriction
and opens the possibility to employ highly expressive latent variable models
like diffusion models. Our approach is conceptually based on a loss that upper
bounds the reverse Kullback-Leibler divergence and evades the requirement of
exact sample likelihoods. We experimentally validate our approach in data-free
Combinatorial Optimization and demonstrate that our method achieves a new
state-of-the-art on a wide range of benchmark problems.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: This paper introduces DiffUCO, a diffusion model framework for unsupervised neural combinatorial optimization. The key contribution is a novel Joint Variational Upper Bound loss function that allows the application of latent variable models like diffusion models to data-free approximation of discrete distributions, achieving state-of-the-art performance on various combinatorial optimization problems.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: The methodology involves training a diffusion model to approximate the Boltzmann distribution associated with a combinatorial optimization problem. The model uses a forward diffusion process to transform the target distribution into a stationary distribution, and a reverse diffusion process, modeled by the neural network, to map samples from the stationary distribution back to the target distribution. The training is based on minimizing a Joint Variational Upper Bound on the reverse Kullback-Leibler divergence. Conditional Expectation (CE) and Subgraph Tokenization (ST) are also used to improve solution quality and efficiency.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The method is evaluated on five different CO problem types: Maximum Independent Set (MIS), Maximum Clique (MaxCl), Minimum Dominating Set (MDS), Maximum Cut (MaxCut), and Minimum Vertex Cover (MVC). The experiments use RB-model graphs for MIS and MaxCl, and Barabasi-Albert (BA) graphs for MDS and MaxCut. Performance is compared against state-of-the-art autoregressive models and classical solvers like Gurobi. Metrics include relative error and approximation ratio. Ablation studies are performed to assess the impact of diffusion steps and noise distributions.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The learned probability distribution has a bias and does not exactly follow the target distribution. Training DiffUCO on large graph datasets with high connectivity is memory- and time-expensive.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research directions include improving memory and time efficiency by reducing the computational graph during backpropagation, exploring latent diffusion models and U-Net inspired GNN architectures, and developing methods that allow to obtain unbiased samples from the target distribution.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title:
Purify++: Improving Diffusion-Purification with Advanced Diffusion
  Models and Control of Randomness
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
Adversarial attacks can mislead neural network classifiers. The defense
against adversarial attacks is important for AI safety. Adversarial
purification is a family of approaches that defend adversarial attacks with
suitable pre-processing. Diffusion models have been shown to be effective for
adversarial purification. Despite their success, many aspects of diffusion
purification still remain unexplored. In this paper, we investigate and improve
upon three limiting designs of diffusion purification: the use of an improved
diffusion model, advanced numerical simulation techniques, and optimal control
of randomness. Based on our findings, we propose Purify++, a new diffusion
purification algorithm that is now the state-of-the-art purification method
against several adversarial attacks. Our work presents a systematic exploration
of the limits of diffusion purification methods.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper introduces Purify++, a new diffusion purification algorithm for defending against adversarial attacks on neural networks. It improves upon existing diffusion purification methods by using an improved diffusion model, advanced numerical simulation techniques, and optimal control of randomness, achieving state-of-the-art purification performance.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: Purify++ employs an improved diffusion model (EDM, a generalization of VE diffusion), efficient purification simulations using Heun's method, and optimal control of randomness achieved by combining purification ODE and Langevin dynamics, controlled by a mixing coefficient.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The method is evaluated on MNIST and CIFAR10 datasets using various adversarial attacks, including black-box (Square Attack, SPSA), gray-box (PGD), and adaptive attacks (BPDA+EOT). Performance is measured using standard and robust accuracy, comparing against existing adversarial defense methods.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The evaluation of the adaptive attack (BPDA+EOT) is performed on a subset of the CIFAR10 test set due to high computational costs. The paper also notes the independent training of the diffusion model and classifier on the same training dataset as previous works, potentially limiting exploration of joint training strategies.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: The paper suggests further developments in diffusion-based purification methods, particularly exploring different architectures and sampling techniques of diffusion models. It also hints at the potential for exploring joint training strategies of the diffusion model and classifier.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title:
Adaptive Learning of the Latent Space of Wasserstein Generative
  Adversarial Networks
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
Generative models based on latent variables, such as generative adversarial
networks (GANs) and variational auto-encoders (VAEs), have gained lots of
interests due to their impressive performance in many fields. However, many
data such as natural images usually do not populate the ambient Euclidean space
but instead reside in a lower-dimensional manifold. Thus an inappropriate
choice of the latent dimension fails to uncover the structure of the data,
possibly resulting in mismatch of latent representations and poor generative
qualities. Towards addressing these problems, we propose a novel framework
called the latent Wasserstein GAN (LWGAN) that fuses the Wasserstein
auto-encoder and the Wasserstein GAN so that the intrinsic dimension of the
data manifold can be adaptively learned by a modified informative latent
distribution. We prove that there exist an encoder network and a generator
network in such a way that the intrinsic dimension of the learned encoding
distribution is equal to the dimension of the data manifold. We theoretically
establish that our estimated intrinsic dimension is a consistent estimate of
the true dimension of the data manifold. Meanwhile, we provide an upper bound
on the generalization error of LWGAN, implying that we force the synthetic data
distribution to be similar to the real data distribution from a population
perspective. Comprehensive empirical experiments verify our framework and show
that LWGAN is able to identify the correct intrinsic dimension under several
scenarios, and simultaneously generate high-quality synthetic data by sampling
from the learned latent distribution.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper proposes a novel framework called Latent Wasserstein GAN (LWGAN) to adaptively learn the intrinsic dimension of data distributions supported on manifolds. LWGAN fuses the Wasserstein auto-encoder and the Wasserstein GAN to improve generative modeling and representation learning by learning a modified informative latent distribution. The paper provides theoretical guarantees on the generalization error bound, estimation consistency, and intrinsic dimension consistency.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: LWGAN combines WGAN and WAE in a principled way. It changes the latent distribution from N(0, Id) to a generalized normal distribution N(0, A), where A is a diagonal matrix with entries taking values 0 or 1, allowing the rank of A to characterize the intrinsic dimension of the latent space. A deterministic encoder Q: X -> Z learns an informative prior distribution PZ ~ N(0, A), and a generator G: Z -> X generates images using the latent code Z from PZ. The 1-Wasserstein distance measures the similarities between distributions.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The framework is verified using simulated experiments on toy examples (S-curve, Swiss roll, Hyperplane) and real image data (MNIST, CelebA). The performance is evaluated based on the ability to detect the correct intrinsic dimension, generate high-quality samples, and obtain small reconstruction errors. Metrics such as Inception Score (IS), Fréchet Inception Distance (FID), and reconstruction error are used.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The paper focuses on a deterministic generator G. The choice of the regularization parameter λn in the rank-regularized objective function requires careful tuning, although a practical data-driven scheme is proposed. The neural network function space G × Q × F needs to be large enough to cover some optimal points of interest, and the theoretical rates for model selection consistency differ from those of BIC.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research directions include investigating a more general scenario where the generator G is stochastic by adding an extra noise vector to its input. Another direction involves incorporating the stochastic LWGAN into more recent GAN modules like BigGAN to produce high-resolution and high-fidelity images along with intrinsic dimension estimation. LWGAN can also be applied to structural estimation in econometrics by adaptively learning data representation and boosting sample size.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title: Hyperbolic Geometric Latent Diffusion Model for Graph Generation
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
Diffusion models have made significant contributions to computer vision,
sparking a growing interest in the community recently regarding the application
of them to graph generation. Existing discrete graph diffusion models exhibit
heightened computational complexity and diminished training efficiency. A
preferable and natural way is to directly diffuse the graph within the latent
space. However, due to the non-Euclidean structure of graphs is not isotropic
in the latent space, the existing latent diffusion models effectively make it
difficult to capture and preserve the topological information of graphs. To
address the above challenges, we propose a novel geometrically latent diffusion
framework HypDiff. Specifically, we first establish a geometrically latent
space with interpretability measures based on hyperbolic geometry, to define
anisotropic latent diffusion processes for graphs. Then, we propose a
geometrically latent diffusion process that is constrained by both radial and
angular geometric properties, thereby ensuring the preservation of the original
topological properties in the generative graphs. Extensive experimental results
demonstrate the superior effectiveness of HypDiff for graph generation with
various topologies.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper introduces HypDiff, a novel hyperbolic geometric latent diffusion model for graph generation that addresses the anisotropy of non-Euclidean structures. It proposes a geometrically latent diffusion process based on radial and angular geometric constraints in hyperbolic space to preserve topological properties and improve generation quality.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: The HypDiff model uses a hyperbolic autoencoder to embed graphs into a hyperbolic space. It then applies a geometrically latent diffusion process with radial and angular constraints. The radial constraint addresses the additivity issue of Gaussian distributions in hyperbolic space, while the angular constraint preserves structural prior during diffusion. A denoising network based on UNET is trained to reverse the diffusion process.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The model was evaluated on synthetic datasets (SBM, BA, Community, Ego, BA-G, Grid) and real-world datasets (Cora, Citeseer, Polblogs, MUTAG, IMDB-B, PROTEINS, COLLAB) for node classification and graph generation tasks. Performance was measured using F1 scores for node classification and Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) and F1 scores (precision-recall, density-coverage) for graph generation. Baselines included Euclidean and hyperbolic graph representation learning methods, deep graph generative models, and graph diffusion generative models.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The paper does not explicitly state limitations, but it can be inferred that the model's performance might be sensitive to the choice of hyperbolic curvature and clustering parameters. Additionally, the approximation of Gaussian distribution in hyperbolic space with tangent plane Gaussian distribution could introduce inaccuracies. The complexity analysis suggests potential scalability issues with very large graphs.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research could explore adaptive selection of hyperbolic curvature and clustering parameters, investigate more accurate approximations of Gaussian distributions in hyperbolic space, and extend the model to handle dynamic graphs or graphs with node/edge features. Applying HypDiff to other graph-related tasks, such as graph classification or link prediction, could also be a promising direction.

The key improvements focus on using Variance Exploding (VE) diffusion (specifically, EDM) instead of Variance Preserving (VP) diffusion, employing Heun's method for efficient simulation, and controlling the strength of randomness in the purification procedure by combining purification ODE and Langevin dynamics.","experimental_setup":"The experiments were conducted on MNIST and CIFAR10 datasets. The performance of Purify++ was evaluated against black-box (Square Attack, SPSA), gray-box (PGD), and adaptive (BPDA+EOT) attacks. Standard and robust accuracy were used as evaluation metrics. The method was compared with existing adversarial training and purification methods, including DiffPure. Ablation studies were performed to quantify the contribution of each component of Purify++.","limitations":"The paper acknowledges the high computational cost associated with strong adaptive attacks like BPDA+EOT, which limits the evaluation to a subset of the test set. Also, some experimental results are marked as being obtained on a subset from the whole test dataset. Further, the specific conditions under which the theoretical guarantees hold for diffusion purification are only mentioned generally.","future_research_directions":"The paper suggests further developments in diffusion-based purification methods, implying potential research directions in exploring new diffusion models, simulation techniques, and randomness control strategies. A specific direction is not explicitly stated but is implied through the conclusion."}

{"arxiv_id":"2404.10177v2","arxiv_url":"http://arxiv.org/abs/2404.10177v2","title":"Consistent Diffusion Meets Tweedie: Training Exact Ambient Diffusion
  Models with Noisy Data","authors":["Giannis Daras","Alexandros G. Dimakis","Constantinos Daskalakis"],"published_date":"2024-03-20T14:22:12Z","journal":"","doi":"","summary":"Ambient diffusion is a recently proposed framework for training diffusion
models using corrupted data. Both Ambient Diffusion and alternative SURE-based
approaches for learning diffusion models from corrupted data resort to
approximations which deteriorate performance. We present the first framework
for training diffusion models that provably sample from the uncorrupted
distribution given only noisy training data, solving an open problem in this
space. Our key technical contribution is a method that uses a double
application of Tweedie's formula and a consistency loss function that allows us
to extend sampling at noise levels below the observed data noise. We also
provide further evidence that diffusion models memorize from their training
sets by identifying extremely corrupted images that are almost perfectly
reconstructed, raising copyright and privacy concerns. Our method for training
using corrupted samples can be used to mitigate this problem. We demonstrate
this by fine-tuning Stable Diffusion XL to generate samples from a distribution
using only noisy samples. Our framework reduces the amount of memorization of
the fine-tuning dataset, while maintaining competitive performance.","github_url":"https://github.com/giannisdaras/ambient-tweedie","main_contributions":"The paper introduces a framework for training diffusion models using only corrupted samples, addressing an open problem in Ambient Diffusion. Key contributions include a method using a double application of Tweedie's formula and a consistency loss function to enable sampling at noise levels below the observed data noise. The research also provides evidence of diffusion models memorizing training sets and uses corrupted samples to mitigate this, fine-tuning Stable Diffusion XL to generate samples from a distribution using only noisy samples and reducing memorization.","methodology":"The methodology involves a double application of Tweedie's formula to learn optimal denoisers for noise levels above that of the training data and a consistency loss function to learn optimal denoisers for noise levels below that of the training data. The approach trains the network to predict the denoised image by removing additional noise introduced to the given samples, using a Stochastic Differential Equation for sampling.","experimental_setup":"Experiments were conducted using Stable Diffusion XL (SDXL) on a subset of the LAION dataset and FFHQ dataset. The models were fine-tuned on corrupted data with varying noise levels. Evaluation metrics included image similarity scores using DINOv2 embeddings and FID score, as well as latent and pixel MSE for restoration performance.","limitations":"The method does not solve the problem of training diffusion models with linearly corrupted data that provably sample from the uncorrupted distribution. Training with consistency increases training time. In preliminary experiments on very limited datasets (< 100 samples), the proposed Ambient Denoising Score Matching objective did not work.","future_research_directions":"Future research directions include exploring training diffusion models with linearly corrupted data that provably sample from the uncorrupted distribution, reducing the training time associated with consistency training, and addressing the limitations observed on very limited datasets."}

{"arxiv_id":"2406.01661v2","arxiv_url":"http://arxiv.org/abs/2406.01661v2","title":"A Diffusion Model Framework for Unsupervised Neural Combinatorial
  Optimization","authors":["Sebastian Sanokowski","Sepp Hochreiter","Sebastian Lehner"],"published_date":"2024-06-03T17:55:02Z","journal":"","doi":"","summary":"Learning to sample from intractable distributions over discrete sets without
relying on corresponding training data is a central problem in a wide range of
fields, including Combinatorial Optimization. Currently, popular deep
learning-based approaches rely primarily on generative models that yield exact
sample likelihoods. This work introduces a method that lifts this restriction
and opens the possibility to employ highly expressive latent variable models
like diffusion models. Our approach is conceptually based on a loss that upper
bounds the reverse Kullback-Leibler divergence and evades the requirement of
exact sample likelihoods. We experimentally validate our approach in data-free
Combinatorial Optimization and demonstrate that our method achieves a new
state-of-the-art on a wide range of benchmark problems.","github_url":"https://github.com/ml-jku/DIffUCO","main_contributions":"This paper introduces DiffUCO, a diffusion model framework for unsupervised neural combinatorial optimization. The key contribution is a novel Joint Variational Upper Bound loss function that allows the application of latent variable models like diffusion models to data-free approximation of discrete distributions, achieving state-of-the-art performance on various combinatorial optimization problems.","methodology":"The methodology involves training a diffusion model to approximate the Boltzmann distribution associated with a combinatorial optimization problem. The model uses a forward diffusion process to transform the target distribution into a stationary distribution, and a reverse diffusion process, modeled by the neural network, to map samples from the stationary distribution back to the target distribution. The training is based on minimizing a Joint Variational Upper Bound on the reverse Kullback-Leibler divergence. Conditional Expectation (CE) and Subgraph Tokenization (ST) are also used to improve solution quality and efficiency.","experimental_setup":"The method is evaluated on five different CO problem types: Maximum Independent Set (MIS), Maximum Clique (MaxCl), Minimum Dominating Set (MDS), Maximum Cut (MaxCut), and Minimum Vertex Cover (MVC). The experiments use RB-model graphs for MIS and MaxCl, and Barabasi-Albert (BA) graphs for MDS and MaxCut. Performance is compared against state-of-the-art autoregressive models and classical solvers like Gurobi. Metrics include relative error and approximation ratio. Ablation studies are performed to assess the impact of diffusion steps and noise distributions.","limitations":"The learned probability distribution has a bias and does not exactly follow the target distribution. Training DiffUCO on large graph datasets with high connectivity is memory- and time-expensive.","future_research_directions":"Future research directions include improving memory and time efficiency by reducing the computational graph during backpropagation, exploring latent diffusion models and U-Net inspired GNN architectures, and developing methods that allow to obtain unbiased samples from the target distribution."}

{"arxiv_id":"2405.03188v1","arxiv_url":"http://arxiv.org/abs/2405.03188v1","title":"Hyperbolic Geometric Latent Diffusion Model for Graph Generation","authors":["Xingcheng Fu","Yisen Gao","Yuecen Wei","Qingyun Sun","Hao Peng","Jianxin Li","Xianxian Li"],"published_date":"2024-05-06T06:28:44Z","journal":"","doi":"","summary":"Diffusion models have made significant contributions to computer vision,
sparking a growing interest in the community recently regarding the application
of them to graph generation. Existing discrete graph diffusion models exhibit
heightened computational complexity and diminished training efficiency. A
preferable and natural way is to directly diffuse the graph within the latent
space. However, due to the non-Euclidean structure of graphs is not isotropic
in the latent space, the existing latent diffusion models effectively make it
difficult to capture and preserve the topological information of graphs. To
address the above challenges, we propose a novel geometrically latent diffusion
framework HypDiff. Specifically, we first establish a geometrically latent
space with interpretability measures based on hyperbolic geometry, to define
anisotropic latent diffusion processes for graphs. Then, we propose a
geometrically latent diffusion process that is constrained by both radial and
angular geometric properties, thereby ensuring the preservation of the original
topological properties in the generative graphs. Extensive experimental results
demonstrate the superior effectiveness of HypDiff for graph generation with
various topologies.","github_url":"https://github.com/RingBDStack/HypDiff","main_contributions":"The paper introduces HypDiff, a novel hyperbolic geometric latent diffusion model for graph generation that addresses the anisotropy of non-Euclidean structures. It proposes a geometrically latent diffusion process based on radial and angular geometric constraints in hyperbolic space to preserve topological properties and improve generation quality.","methodology":"The HypDiff model uses a hyperbolic autoencoder to embed graphs into a hyperbolic space. It then applies a geometrically latent diffusion process with radial and angular constraints. The radial constraint addresses the additivity issue of Gaussian distributions in hyperbolic space, while the angular constraint preserves structural prior during diffusion. A denoising network based on UNET is trained to reverse the diffusion process.","experimental_setup":"The model was evaluated on synthetic datasets (SBM, BA, Community, Ego, BA-G, Grid) and real-world datasets (Cora, Citeseer, Polblogs, MUTAG, IMDB-B, PROTEINS, COLLAB) for node classification and graph generation tasks. Performance was measured using F1 scores for node classification and Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) and F1 scores (precision-recall, density-coverage) for graph generation. Baselines included Euclidean and hyperbolic graph representation learning methods, deep graph generative models, and graph diffusion generative models.","limitations":"The paper does not explicitly state limitations, but it can be inferred that the model's performance might be sensitive to the choice of hyperbolic curvature and clustering parameters.
